name: WebSocket handshake accepts any Origin (Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking)
description: Performs the WebSocket upgrade handshake with an attacker-controlled Origin header. When the server completes the handshake (101 Switching Protocols) regardless of Origin, it does not validate where the connection request came from — so any web page the victim visits can open an authenticated WebSocket to this endpoint using the victim's ambient credentials (cookies) and read/drive the live channel: Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking. Unlike CORS, the same-origin policy does NOT protect WebSocket handshakes, so the server must check Origin itself. The predicate matches on the 101 status returned for a cross-origin handshake.

vulnerability.remediation: Validate the Origin header on the WebSocket handshake against a server-side allow-list before accepting the upgrade. ASP.NET Core: `app.UseWebSockets(new WebSocketOptions { AllowedOrigins = { "https://app.example.com" } })` (the middleware then rejects other origins with a 403), or check `context.Request.Headers.Origin` in the endpoint and return 403. Do NOT rely on cookies/SameSite alone. Additionally bind auth to a per-connection token in the handshake rather than ambient cookies. Verify with a handshake carrying a foreign Origin — it should return 403, not 101.
